what_hurts_and_how_long_has_it_hurt: Lower back pain centered around my lumbar spine (mostly on the right side, sometimes radiating into my right hip and down the back of my right leg), along with stiffness in my neck and occasional numbness/tingling in my right foot. The lower back pain has been consistently present for about 2 years, with milder, on-and-off episodes for roughly 5–6 years before that. The neck stiffness has been more noticeable for the last 10–12 months. The numbness in my right foot started about 8–9 months ago and now happens a few times per week, especially after sitting for long periods or driving more than 30–40 minutes.
when_do_you_think_these_problems_origina: I first remember my lower back flaring up badly around 2019-03-15 after helping a friend move—lots of heavy lifting, twisting, and going up and down stairs all day. Before that I had occasional soreness from desk work, but it was more of an annoyance. The pain became more frequent and more intense during 2022, especially when I started working from home full-time and using a non-ergonomic setup at my kitchen table. The consistent daily pain really started around 2023-01-01, and the numbness/tingling in my right foot began to show up around 2024-03-01.
if_yes_what_were_you_told:
I’ve seen my primary care provider twice and a physical therapist for about 8 sessions. My primary care doctor told me that based on my symptoms and exam, it sounded like chronic mechanical low back pain with possible mild nerve involvement, likely related to poor posture, weak core muscles, and maybe a small disc bulge or irritation around L4-L5 or L5-S1. She did not think it was an emergency but recommended imaging if it worsened. She also mentioned that my weight gain during the pandemic and long hours sitting at a computer were contributing factors.

The physical therapist said my hip flexors and hamstrings were very tight and that I had weak glute muscles and core instability. He also noted that my posture (forward head, rounded shoulders, and slight anterior pelvic tilt) was putting extra strain on my lower back. He believed that with consistent exercise, stretching, and posture changes, the pain could be significantly reduced. He did not see any signs of severe nerve damage at that time but was concerned about the numbness and suggested that if it progressed, I should get an MRI.

An urgent care visit in late 2023, when I had a severe flare-up, resulted in them telling me it was likely an acute flare of a chronic low back issue. They gave me a short course of muscle relaxers and NSAIDs and recommended rest, heat/ice, and follow-up with my primary care provider.
what_was_done:
1. Primary care visits: My doctor did a physical exam (range of motion tests, reflexes, strength, and sensation). She prescribed a prescription-strength NSAID (meloxicam) for short-term use and recommended over-the-counter ibuprofen as needed after that. She also suggested using a heating pad, gentle stretching, and avoiding heavy lifting for a few weeks. She referred me to physical therapy.

2. Physical therapy: I completed about 8 sessions over roughly 10 weeks. The PT sessions included:
- Core strengthening exercises (planks, dead bugs, bird-dogs)
- Hip and hamstring stretches
- Glute activation exercises (bridges, clamshells, side steps with resistance band)
- Postural training (how to sit at my desk, how to stand, how to bend and lift correctly)
- Manual therapy (soft tissue work on my lower back and hips, some gentle mobilizations)
- A home exercise program, which I was supposed to do 4–5 times per week

3. Medications and self-care:
- Short course of muscle relaxers (cyclobenzaprine) from urgent care during a severe flare-up in 2023.
- NSAIDs like ibuprofen as needed, usually 400–600 mg once or twice a day during bad days.
- Occasional use of over-the-counter lidocaine patches on my lower back.
- Ice packs during acute flare-ups and a heating pad in the evenings a few times per week.

4. Lifestyle modifications:
- I bought an adjustable office chair with lumbar support and a laptop stand to raise my screen, plus an external keyboard and mouse.
- I started using a standing desk converter so I can alternate between sitting and standing.
- I tried to walk more regularly, aiming for 20–30 minutes a few times per week.

5. Imaging and referrals:
- No MRI yet. My doctor has mentioned it as a next step if symptoms progress or if I develop weakness or more significant numbness.
- No surgery or injections so far.
how_does_this_impact_your_life:
This affects almost every part of my daily life.

Family: I have a 7-year-old daughter and a 4-year-old son. I used to pick them up and carry them around the house or on my shoulders at the park, but now I avoid lifting them because I’m afraid of triggering a flare-up. It makes me feel guilty when my kids ask me to pick them up and I have to say no or sit down first and have them climb onto my lap. My spouse has taken on more of the physically demanding chores (like vacuuming, taking out the trash, and carrying laundry baskets), which sometimes causes tension because they feel overburdened and I feel like I’m not pulling my weight.

Work: I work full-time as a project manager in IT, mostly from home. I sit in front of a computer for 8–9 hours a day. The pain makes it hard to sit comfortably for more than 20–30 minutes at a time. I find myself constantly shifting positions, standing up, or stretching during meetings. I’ve had to turn off my camera in video calls more often because I’m standing or lying on the floor to relieve my back. On really bad days, it’s harder to focus and I feel more irritable and mentally fatigued. I worry that my productivity and reliability might be slipping, even though no one has formally complained.

Social life: I’ve become more hesitant to make social plans that involve standing for long periods (like concerts, museum trips, or going out to crowded events). I often suggest low-key activities like sitting at a coffee shop or having friends over instead of going out. I’ve turned down invitations to things like hiking trips or pickup soccer games because I’m afraid I’ll either be in pain the whole time or make my back worse. This has made me feel more isolated and a bit older than I actually am (I’m only in my late 30s).

Sleep: Nights are tough. If I lie flat on my back for too long, my lower back stiffens up and sometimes the tingling in my leg gets worse. I end up sleeping with a pillow under my knees or curled slightly on my side with a pillow between my legs. I wake up at least 2–3 times a night to change positions or because my back is aching. As a result, I often wake up feeling tired and not fully rested, which then affects my mood and energy the next day.

Exercise and movement: Before all this, I used to jog 2–3 miles a few times a week and occasionally do yoga classes. I’ve basically stopped running because the impact tends to make my back tighten up for days afterward. I’ve switched to walking, but even that can be challenging if I go longer than 30–40 minutes without a break. I’m reluctant to go back to a gym because I’m afraid of doing something wrong and making it worse, and I feel self-conscious about not being able to do things I used to do easily.

Chores: Simple tasks like loading the dishwasher, doing laundry, or cleaning the bathtub now require planning. I have to bend at the knees and hips carefully, and I try not to twist while holding anything heavy. If I spend a couple of hours doing weekend chores, my back often feels tight and sore for the rest of the day. I’ve also noticed that carrying groceries from the car to the apartment can trigger pain, so I now make more trips with lighter bags instead of fewer trips with heavier loads.

Focus and mood: Chronic discomfort makes it harder to stay focused on work or conversations. I find myself thinking about my back a lot—anticipating which movement might hurt, worrying about whether it’s getting worse, and replaying what I “should” be doing differently. This constant background worry has made me more anxious and a bit more withdrawn. I’m more irritable with my family when I’m in pain, and afterward I feel guilty about snapping at them.

Self-image and self-esteem: I feel older and less capable than I think I should at this stage of my life. I’ve gained about 15 pounds over the last few years, partly from being less active, and I don’t feel great about how I look. Not being able to do simple physical things—like playing on the floor with my kids for a long time, lifting a suitcase without thinking, or going for a spontaneous run—makes me feel like my body is failing me. I also sometimes feel embarrassed when I have to stand up and stretch in the middle of a meeting or sit on the floor at friends’ houses.

Play and recreation: I used to enjoy weekend hikes, casual pickup basketball, and longer walks around the city. Now, I limit those activities a lot. When my kids ask me to play tag or roughhouse, I have to be cautious and often redirect them to board games or seated activities. I miss feeling physically free and spontaneous.

Walking: I can still walk, but I plan my routes and make sure there are places to sit or rest. If I walk more than about 30–40 minutes without a break, I start to feel pressure in my lower back and sometimes mild tingling in my right leg. Uneven ground or going downhill tends to bother my back more.

Overall health concerns: I worry that this is going to continue to get worse as I get older and that I might eventually need surgery or stronger medications. I’m concerned about relying too much on painkillers and about how this chronic pain might affect my mental health over time. It’s also made me more aware of my posture, weight, and general fitness, but I feel stuck between wanting to exercise more and being afraid of aggravating my back.
are_you_doing_anything_differently_becau:
Yes, I’ve made a lot of changes in my daily routines because of this.

At work: I set a timer to remind myself to stand up and move around every 25–30 minutes. I alternate between sitting and standing using a standing desk converter. I’ve adjusted my chair and monitor height so that my screen is at eye level and my feet are flat on the floor. I try not to work from the couch or bed anymore, even though it’s tempting, because I notice that my back gets worse when I do.

At home: I’m very careful about how I lift things—bending my knees, keeping items close to my body, and avoiding twisting while holding weight. I break tasks into smaller chunks; for example, instead of doing all the cleaning in one afternoon, I spread it out over several days. I avoid carrying both kids at the same time and try not to carry my 4-year-old for long distances. I also keep a small step stool in the kitchen so I don’t have to reach and twist awkwardly for items on high shelves.

Sleep habits: I’ve changed my sleeping positions and now usually sleep on my side with a pillow between my knees or on my back with a pillow under my knees. I added a mattress topper to make the bed slightly firmer and more supportive. I also keep a heating pad near the bed and sometimes use it for 15–20 minutes before sleep.

Exercise: I no longer run or do high-impact workouts. Instead, I focus on walking, light stretching, and some of the exercises the physical therapist gave me (although I admit I’m not as consistent as I should be). I avoid heavy weightlifting and any exercises that involve a lot of bending and twisting, like certain ab workouts. I’ve also been more cautious with yard work or any activity that involves repetitive bending.

Driving and commuting: I use a small lumbar support cushion in the car and try not to drive more than 30–40 minutes without a break. If we go on a longer road trip, I plan stops so I can get out, stretch, and walk around for a few minutes.

Social and leisure activities: I tend to choose seating with back support when we go out (e.g., chairs instead of bar stools, booths instead of benches without backs). I avoid activities that require standing for long periods, like long lines or crowded standing-room events. I also sometimes decline invitations that I think will involve a lot of walking or carrying things.

General habits: I’ve become more mindful of my posture when sitting, standing, and even brushing my teeth or washing dishes. I try to engage my core when lifting or bending. I also keep over-the-counter pain medication and a reusable ice pack handy so I can manage flare-ups quickly.
anything_else:
Emotionally, this has been harder than I expected. I always thought of back pain as something that happened to “older” people or people who had serious injuries, and I didn’t really imagine it would be something I’d have to manage in my 30s. I feel like I’ve tried a mix of things—PT, stretches, better ergonomics, meds—and while they’ve helped to some degree, I don’t feel like I’ve gotten to the root of the problem or achieved a stable, manageable routine. I often feel like I’m just reacting to flare-ups rather than actually improving.

I also notice that when my stress levels go up (for example, during busy project deadlines at work or during family conflicts), my back pain seems to get worse. It makes me think there’s a strong mind-body connection here, but I’m not entirely sure what to do about it. I sometimes worry that other people think I’m exaggerating or using my back pain as an excuse, even though I know I’m genuinely limited by it.

I would really like a more comprehensive plan that helps me understand exactly what’s going on physically (e.g., whether there is a disc issue) and gives me a clear, realistic path to getting stronger and more functional again. I’m open to doing the work—exercises, lifestyle changes, etc.—I just need guidance and a sense that it’s actually going to move me in the right direction. I also want to learn what’s safe versus what I should truly avoid, so I don’t hold back unnecessarily out of fear.

Finally, I’m worried about setting a good example for my kids. I don’t want them to see me as fragile or always in pain, but I also don’t want to pretend everything is fine and then end up hurting myself more. I’d like to be able to be active with them, go on hikes, play at the park, and travel without constantly thinking about my back.
